blocks.body: Future Perfect shows what will already be finished by a future point, and Future Perfect Continuous shows what will have been happening for some time up to that future point.

{"level":"B2","intro":"In this topic, you compare Future Perfect and Future Perfect Continuous: are we asking what will already be finished by that point or how long the action will have been going on by that point?","rules":[{"label":"FUTURE PERFECT","color":"violet","text":"will have + V3 for a completed result by a future limit or deadline.","example":"By next week, I'll have finished the report. / By the time you arrive, they'll have left."},{"label":"FUTURE PERFECT CONTINUOUS","color":"rose","text":"will have been + V-ing for duration or process up to a future point.","example":"By noon, I'll have been working for two hours. / By next month, she'll have been living here for ten years."},{"label":"KEY QUESTION","color":"blue","text":"Ask yourself: what will already be finished by then? or how long will it have been happening by then?","example":"completed result / duration up to a future point"}]}

{"title":"1. Quick comparison","intro":"Both forms look from a future viewpoint, but they ask different questions:","rows":[{"en":"Future Perfect","ua":"what will already be complete by that point","note":"By then, we'll have solved the problem."},{"en":"Future Perfect Continuous","ua":"how long the process will have been continuing by that point","note":"By then, we'll have been working on it for two hours."},{"en":"by then / by next week / by the time","ua":"often points to a finished result","note":"By the time she arrives, I'll have cleaned the flat."},{"en":"for two hours / for ten years / how long","ua":"often points to duration or process","note":"How long will you have been waiting by then?"},{"en":"result vs duration","ua":"finished outcome vs ongoing process up to the limit","note":"will have finished vs will have been working"}],"warning":"If the main idea is a completed result before a deadline, choose Future Perfect. If the main idea is duration or process up to a deadline, choose Future Perfect Continuous."}

{"title":"2. Forms grid","intro":"Compare the basic affirmative, negative, and question patterns.","items":[{"label":"Future Perfect","title":"Affirmative","subtitle":"Subject + will have + V3: I will have finished."},{"label":"Future Perfect","title":"Negative","subtitle":"Subject + won't have + V3: I won't have finished."},{"label":"Future Perfect","title":"Question","subtitle":"Will + subject + have + V3? Will you have finished by then?"},{"label":"Future Perfect Continuous","title":"Affirmative","subtitle":"Subject + will have been + V-ing: I will have been working."},{"label":"Future Perfect Continuous","title":"Negative","subtitle":"Subject + won't have been + V-ing: I won't have been working."},{"label":"Future Perfect Continuous","title":"Question","subtitle":"Will + subject + have been + V-ing? How long will you have been waiting by then?"}]}

{"title":"3. When the meaning changes","sections":[{"label":"A completed result by the limit","color":"violet","description":"Choose Future Perfect when you want to stress that the action will already be complete by a future point.","examples":[{"en":"By next week, I'll have finished the report.","ua":"The result will already be ready by next week."},{"en":"By the time you call, she'll have sent the email.","ua":"One action will finish before the other future event."}]},{"label":"Duration up to the limit","color":"rose","description":"Choose Future Perfect Continuous when it matters how long the action will have been continuing by a future point, or when the process leads up to that point.","examples":[{"en":"By noon, I'll have been working for two hours.","ua":"The focus is on duration before noon."},{"en":"By next month, she'll have been teaching here for ten years.","ua":"The focus is on a long period up to a future point."},{"en":"How long will you have been waiting by the time the train arrives?","ua":"The question directly asks for duration, not a finished result."}]},{"label":"A paired contrast","color":"blue","description":"The context is similar, but the focus changes: what will be finished or how long the process will have been continuing by the same limit.","examples":[{"en":"By Friday, they'll have completed the bridge repairs.","ua":"We ask about the finished result before the deadline."},{"en":"By Friday, they'll have been working on the bridge for ten days.","ua":"We ask about the duration of the process before the same deadline."}],"note":"by then / by next week / by the time often points to Future Perfect, while for two hours / for ten years / how long often points to Future Perfect Continuous."}]}

{"title":"4. Typical mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"violet","title":"Using Future Perfect Continuous when a finished result is needed","wrong":"❌ By next week, I will have been finishing the report.","right":"✅ By next week, I will have finished the report."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"rose","title":"Using Future Perfect when the question is about duration","wrong":"❌ By noon, she will have studied for two hours.","right":"✅ By noon, she will have been studying for two hours."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"blue","title":"Ignoring the question how long?","wrong":"❌ How long will you have finished by then?","right":"✅ How long will you have been working by then?"},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"amber","title":"Breaking the pattern with been or V3","wrong":"❌ will have been finished the task / will have finish","right":"✅ will have been working / will have finished"}]}

{"title":"5. A short decision guide","items":["Do you need a finished result before a future limit? → choose Future Perfect.","Do you need duration or process up to a future limit? → choose Future Perfect Continuous.","If you have by then / by next week / by the time and the question is 'what will be ready?', you often need will have + V3.","If you have for two hours / for ten years or the question how long?, you often need will have been + V-ing.","If you are unsure, say the contrast out loud: finished result or ongoing duration up to that point?"]}
